steps.title: Let Them Build the Center
steps.text:
The **King's Indian Defense** (KID) is one of the most uncompromising openings ever invented. Black says: *take the center, build whatever you like — and then watch me tear it down*.

It was the lifelong weapon of **Garry Kasparov**, **Bobby Fischer**, **David Bronstein**, and many other fighters. When you play the King's Indian, you're not playing for a draw — you're playing to win, even if it's risky.

## How you reach it

The defining sequence is {{d4}} **{{Nf6}}** {{c4}} **{{g6}}** {{Nc3}} **{{Bg7}}**. Black fianchettoes the king's bishop and lets White build a massive center.

## What each side wants

- **White's plan:** complete the big center with {{e4}}, develop with {{Nf3}}, **{{Be2}}** (or Be3, h3 in the Sämisch), castle short, and either lock the center for a queenside attack or break with **c5/d5**.
- **Black's plan:** finish development with **...0-0**, **...d6**, **...Nbd7** or **...Nc6**, and play the central thrust **...e5**. After White locks with **d5**, Black launches **...f5-f4-f3** kingside pawn storms while White attacks the queenside with **c5**.
- **Pawn structure:** the *King's Indian Mar del Plata* — locked center with d5 vs e5, pieces redeployed to opposite wings, pawn races on both sides.

## The signature theme

In classical King's Indian middlegames, the question becomes: *whose attack arrives first?* If White breaks through on the queenside, the game ends. If Black's kingside avalanche reaches the white king, the game ends. There are very few quiet positions.

> The King's Indian: bring a chess set, leave with a war story.

steps.title: The Classical Main Line
steps.text:
After {{d4}} {{Nf6}} {{c4}} {{g6}} {{Nc3}} {{Bg7}} {{e4}} **d6** {{Nf3}} **0-0**, you've reached the **Classical King's Indian** — one of the most-analyzed positions in chess.

## The branching point

White chooses among several major systems on move 6:

- **6.Be2** — *Classical Main Line*. Quiet development. Black plays **...e5**, and after **7.0-0 Nc6 8.d5 Ne7**, Black is set up for the famous **kingside attack**.
- **6.h3** — *Mäkagonov System*. Prevents ...Bg4 pins, prepares Be3.
- **6.f3** — *Sämisch Variation*. Aggressive: White prepares Be3, Qd2, 0-0-0, and a queenside attack with sharp pawn pushes.
- **6.Bg5** — *Averbakh Variation*. Pin the f6 knight, discourage ...e5.
- **6.g3** — *Fianchetto Variation*. White also fianchettoes, leading to long, strategic battles.

## A classic Mar del Plata pattern

After **6.Be2 e5 7.0-0 Nc6 8.d5 Ne7 9.Ne1 Nd7 10.Be3 f5 11.f3 f4 12.Bf2 g5** — the famous pawn-storm formation. Black plans **...g4-g3** to crack White's king. White plans **c5-c6**, **Rb1**, **b4-b5** to crack Black's queenside. May the faster attacker win.

## Why grandmasters fear it

The King's Indian creates **mutual zugzwang on attack speed**. White is *objectively better* in many lines — but a single tempo loss leads to mate. That's why even Kasparov said he eventually stopped playing it.

> Both kings are in mortal danger from move 15. That's not a bug — that's the feature.

steps.title: Why It's Hard
steps.text:
The King's Indian is one of the most *demanding* openings to play well. The reasons are concrete.

## What makes it hard for Black

- **One bad pawn move can collapse the kingside.** Push **...f5** at the wrong moment and the e6 square becomes a hole forever.
- **The bishop pair** matters — losing the dark-squared bishop on g7 (the heart of the position) often dooms the attack.
- **Calculation depth.** Mar del Plata races demand 10-move-deep tactics on both wings simultaneously.
- **No second chances.** If White's queenside attack arrives one move first, the game is lost — no slow regrouping like in the French.

## What makes it rewarding

- **Imbalance from move 5.** No symmetric draws.
- **Attacks on the king.** Few openings produce as many beautiful checkmates as the King's Indian.
- **Strategic richness.** You learn pawn breaks, piece manoeuvring, attack-and-defense, all in one opening.

## Choosing the King's Indian for yourself

If you love **dynamic imbalance**, **mutual attacks**, and **calculated risk**, the King's Indian is the best opening on the planet. If you prefer slow, technical play, choose the Slav or Caro-Kann instead.

> The King's Indian is a black belt's opening. Train hard, fight harder.
